## [00:00 - 03:03]

In this lesson, we'll discuss an acquisition sweet spot, so to speak. For context, I have a proprietary research project on a hundred different industries and what valuations they trade for. For context, I have proprietary research over the last 11 years on a hundred different industries that I paid over $5 million for. Because of what I paid for it and the depth of research that we did, I can't really expose or share the information. But what I can do is I can give you highlights and how I thought about running the analysis so that you can do the same thing for businesses in your market. The whole point here is to find high-valued businesses that will give you an amazing return on investment. And while I can't share this data outright, I can distill some of the best industries that we found that research points to as an acquisition target that might be right for you. I believe these will be the most attractive sectors to target in terms of upside potential in business. Now, before I go any further, I'm not suggesting you jump at these industries, especially if they don't fit your criteria and if you don't have any expertise in those particular businesses. I'm also not saying you can't profitably acquire and scale businesses and sectors outside of this list because obviously you can. However, if you do have expertise in one of these sectors, they're extremely attractive businesses. These businesses, which I refer to as mom-and-pop micro-market businesses, are some of the best vehicles for buying and selling businesses. And they're things like chiropractic, dental, HVAC, multifamily construction, remodeling, security business, cybersecurity, audio-visual businesses, mortgage businesses, insurance businesses, and IT and real estate. All these would be great micro-market businesses that you could build a great business with little to no risk depending on the size business you wanted to start or that you bought. You could buy these businesses for usually two to two and a half times what they pay themselves. I call this ODE, Owner's Discretionary Earnings because I want to look at what they're also running through the business besides what they pay themselves. And you can usually construct those acquisition deals with anywhere from 30 to 40 percent of the purchase price down if the business owner is truly desperate or wants to retire. Usually a distressed seller is experiencing a death or it's a business that's plateaued and they're getting nervous or they're getting tired or they're getting older or their key people left them. All these could be reasons why it's distressed. If you can find a profitable business in one of these spaces that has a distressed seller where the business has not been around too long, that might be a great time to purchase. If you find one that's been around for a long time, it probably has some key employees and you need to watch out because those key employees may be thinking about starting their own business if you buy the business they're in. So sometimes it's good to know how long the business has been in business, how many people
